Peripheral nerve stimulation (TOF) provides objective NMB assessment — electrical stimulus → muscle contraction proportional to {{c1::available (unblocked) nicotinic ACh receptors}}.
Excessive/prolonged NMBA paralysis risks {{c1::ICU-acquired weakness}} and delayed recovery — use the {{c2::lowest}} NMBA dose achieving the clinical goal.

The Train-of-Four delivers 4 electrical stimuli over approximately {{c1::1.5 seconds}}, with a {{c2::0.5-second}} interval between stimuli and a pulse duration of {{c3::0.2 ms}}. Assess twitch count (0–4) and fade (T1→T4).
Ulnar nerve TOF stimulation produces contraction of {{c1::adductor pollicis}}, causing {{c2::thumb adduction}}. Preferred site — lowest risk of direct muscle stimulation.
For ulnar nerve TOF, the distal negative (−) electrode is placed {{c1::over the nerve at the wrist crease}} and the proximal positive (+) electrode {{c2::≥2cm proximal}}.
column2: Black (−) distal, red (+) proximal — standard polarity for all TOF sites

column1: For TOF monitoring, the facial nerve ({{c1::temporal}} branch) produces contraction of {{c2::corrugator supercilii}} (eyebrow movement). More susceptible to direct muscle stimulation than ulnar nerve.
For facial nerve TOF, the distal negative (−) electrode is placed at the {{c1::lateral canthus}} and the proximal positive (+) electrode {{c2::~2cm inferior, near the tragus}}.
The posterior tibial nerve for TOF is found {{c1::posterior to the medial malleolus}} and activates {{c2::flexor hallucis brevis}}, producing {{c3::great toe plantar flexion}}.
Electrode spacing for TOF monitoring should be at least {{c1::2.5 cm}}.
Skin preparation for TOF electrode placement: {{c1::clip hair, clean with alcohol, use gel electrodes}}.
Electrolyte disturbances that alter TOF response: {{c1::↓K⁺, ↓Ca²⁺, ↑Mg²⁺}}.
Supramaximal stimulation is confirmed at {{c1::~20% above the maximal}} stimulus level — the point at which no further increase in twitch height occurs with increasing current.
Supramaximal current must be determined {{c1::<u>before NMBA administration</u>}} to ensure consistent and reliable nerve activation throughout monitoring.
TOF 4/4 equal twitches → {{c1::<70%}} receptor blockade — {{c2::no or minimal}} block.
TOF 4/4 twitches with fade → {{c1::>70%}} receptor blockade — {{c2::partial}} block.
TOF 3/4 twitches → approximately {{c1::75–80%}} receptor blockade.
TOF 2/4 twitches → approximately {{c1::80%}} receptor blockade. Target range for most ICU NMB indications.
TOF 1/4 twitches → approximately {{c1::90%}} receptor blockade — deep block.
TOF 0/4 twitches → approximately {{c1::95%}} receptor blockade — profound block. Must exclude technical failure before assuming full paralysis.
Fade (T4 < T1) on TOF is characteristic of {{c1::non-depolarising}} NMBAs (e.g. rocuronium, vecuronium). Not seen in suxamethonium phase 1 block.
Fade on TOF is caused by {{c1::progressive ACh depletion with each stimulus}} — as the nerve runs low, the NMBA outcompetes ACh more easily, producing progressively weaker T1→T4.
Uniform depression without fade on TOF is characteristic of {{c1::depolarising}} NMBAs — suxamethonium {{c2::phase 1}} block — all four twitches equally depressed regardless of depth.
Very large or repeated doses of suxamethonium cause {{c1::phase 2 block}}, which mimics a {{c2::non-depolarising}} pattern and may produce {{c3::fade}}.
The typical ICU NMBA titration target is {{c1::<u>2/4</u>}} twitches — approximately {{c2::<u>80%</u>}} receptor blockade.
TOF monitoring frequency: initially {{c1::hourly}}, after a dose change or bolus every {{c2::15 minutes}}, stable patient up to {{c3::4-hourly}}.
TOF dose adjustment: too many twitches → {{c1::<u>increase NMBA dose</u>}}
